Channels of Engagement
The Solano Subbasin GSA Collaborative utilized multiple mechanisms to engage with beneficial users of the Solano Subbasin and members of the public. What follows is a summary of the various channels of engagement that have been used to date. Appendix 2a provides details of the Solano Subbasin GSA Due to COVID-19 pandemic social distancing restrictions, public engagement meetings were hosted virtually starting in March 2020; however, meetings were able to be hosted via Zoom video calls and included simultaneous Spanish language translation. Engagement Meetings held in English and translated to Spanish were held to build an awareness of SMGA, provide regular updates on the GSP planning process and public engagement timeline, and help the project team by develop an understanding of the interests, concerns, and questions of key beneficial user groups. Simultaneous Spanish language translation and childcare were offered at each in-person meeting. Notices were sent by email to the interested parties list, via mailed postcards, through local newspaper ads, radio promotion, and through community partners.
The Community Advisory Committee45 1274 (CAC) included 16 volunteers representing beneficial uses and users across the full geography of the subbasin. CAC members provided focused input during the development of the GSP, but did not have decision-making authority. CAC meetings were held and noticed publicly, and observers could attend and offer comments at the end of the meeting.
 GSA Board of Director meetings, scheduled monthly, were open to the public, with time for public comment at each meeting. Solano Subbasin GSA Collaborative meetings have been held since June 2018. Meetings have generally been held once per month, with in person and call-in options for participation by members of the Collaborative. Meeting topics include GSP goals, direction, and scope; stakeholder engagement planning; GSA working agreements (governance); funding updates; and GSP topics. Meeting summaries are included on the Solano GSP website. Newsletter and Interested Parties List: The newsletter was intended to engage and inform the public on the GSP, including public engagement meetings, CAC meetings, and updates to the GSP development. Newsletters were initially sent out on a quarterly basis starting in 2018 and with increasing frequency as GSP development progressed. An example can be found in Appendix 2a. Solano Subbasin Surveys: Several surveys were developed and distributed in paper form at public engagement meetings, and electronically through the Solano GSP website, the newsletter and all other outreach channels. A summary of the results is found in Appendix 2a.Solano GSP Website46 1292 : Meeting results, announcements, deliverables, and surveys were posted as they occurred via the Solano GSP website. Focus Groups on specific aspects of the GSP were held as needed to include technical expertise and topic-dependent stakeholders. A summary can be found in Appendix 2a. Public Forums: The GSA Collaborative, in partnership with the The Freshwater Trust and Local Government Commission, held focused discussions and input sessions around specific topics that required further exploration and engagement. A summary is included in Appendix 2a.

3.3.2 Outreach channel recommendations
There is limited knowledge around where the most groundwater-dependent vulnerable communities are located and how to best engage these groups. Interviewees did provide recommendations for effective outreach strategies to vulnerable communities. Generally, most interviewees were unaware of public awareness campaigns around drinking water, groundwater, and water quality. Both the Dixon Unified School District and the River Delta Unified School District were commonly cited by interviewees as one of the most accessible outreach channels for connecting with families, some of whom may be reliant on groundwater for drinking, cooking, and sanitation or may be using private domestic wells. One interviewee mentioned that parents, especially from the Latinx farm-working community, have communicated a level of respect for the schools, and that any information provided by the schools will be taken seriously by families. River Unified School District allows outside organizations to send District-approved materials home in children’s backpacks every Wednesday, and this may be a
key outreach channel for conducting smaller focus groups in the next phase of engagement. River Unified also offers migrant education programs that may be a helpful avenue for connecting with families for the next steps of outreach.
Libraries were also frequently mentioned as a hub for community engagement, and librarians were viewed as important gatekeepers to sharing information about local events and resources. Utilizing online groups (Facebook, Nextdoor.com, etc.) to engage with community groups was also recommended. Most Solano County librarians are active members on Nextdoor.com, an online community platform, as well as other local Facebook groups; librarians often post on behalf of other organizations to promote events and share resources with the broader network of members. Some local libraries (e.g. Dixon Public Library) also host family resource meetings and other parents groups that were recommended as potential avenues for engaging vulnerable community members. Additional public venues noted for posting flyers for events were local post offices, grocery stores, and local community centers. Rotary Clubs were commonly cited as a convener of groups across the Subbasin and were recommended for additional outreach. Some interviewees also pointed to faith-based groups as a key player to engage
individuals and families that were likely not already captured by GSA Collaborative outreach efforts. Individuals that were identified but not previously engaged in Solano Subbasin groundwater planning efforts were excited to hear about the project and surprised by the resources being offered at the Open House meetings (e.g. translation and childcare services). Interviewees from groups working directly withfamily and children (e.g. Solano Family and Children Services, Child Development Centers and Continuing Development Incorporated, Rio Vista CARE) expressed interest in the project, and two interviewees mentioned that the mostly Latinx, farm-working communities should be engaged, although both notedthe difficulty in engaging these groups, predominantly due to their geographic and linguistic isolation. Most interviewees were unaware of public awareness campaigns around drinking water, groundwater, and water quality.

Community Advisory Committee and Beneficial User Groups
The Community Advisory Committee (CAC) included 17 volunteers representing beneficial uses and users across the full geography and demographics of the Subbasin, including small and large agriculture, rural residential, urban areas, planning, land use and development, local tribal interests, and work on related environmental issues. CAC members were identified by members of the Solano Collaborative and with additional proposals made by LGC and TFT through their outreach and engagement efforts in SDAC communities. CAC members provided focused input during GSP development, but they did not have decision-making authority. Interested members of the general public were invited to join CAC meetings as observers to learn from the presentations and offer comments. CAC meetings were announced and promoted via the Solano SGMA newsletter and website.

INITIAL RESULTS
3.1 Groundwater-dependent SDACs and DACs identified from publicly available datasets The results from the geospatial analysis indicate that there are 11 SDAC communities based on ACS 5- Year Survey MHI (Table 2, Figure 2). The SDACs are distributed across four cities: seven Block Groups within Vacaville (SDAC-1-7), one block group in Dixon (SDAC- 8), two block groups in Rio Vista (SDAC- 9, SDAC-10), and one place in Isleton (SDAC -11). Overall, the total population within SDAC communities in the Solano Subbasin is approximately 13,364 people, or 11% of the total population within the Subbasin. The Northern Delta GSA, Vacaville GSA Solano GSA, and Solano Irrigation District GSA each have at least one SDAC community within their jurisdictions (Figure 2). MHIs range from $23,771 to $39,880 for these communities.

&&

Disadvantaged Communities were also identified within the Subbasin. DAC communities are listed Table 3 and illustrated in Figure 2. The DACs are distributed within three cities: Walnut Grove, Vacaville and Dixon, as well as the southern portion of the Basin. Overall, the total population within SDAC communities in the Solano Subbasin is 24,153 people, or approximately 20% of the total population within the basin. All six of the GSAs within the Subbasin have at least one DAC within their jurisdictions. Median Household Incomes range from $39,538 to $49,267 for these communities. The MHI of the Rio Vista tract that is located at the southern portion of the Subbasin (DAC- A) in Sacramento County is just 1%, or $504/year over the SDAC MHI threshold.

3.3.4 Groundwater Quality Conditions (§ 354.16d) (HCM Section 3.4 & 3.5.9)
This section discusses the historical and current groundwater quality conditions within the Subbasin. Characterization of groundwater conditions in the Subbasin focuses on the following key constituents of concern relating to the beneficial use of groundwater: Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), chloride, nitrate, arsenic, hexavalent chromium (chromium-6), and boron. These constituents have greater potential for presenting broader regional groundwater quality concerns in the Subbasin extending beyond localized or site‐specific conditions. All of these constituents are naturally occurring in the environment, although their occurrence and concentrations in groundwater can be affected by anthropogenic (i.e., human influenced) activities. Appendix 3-A Figures 3-21 through 3-27 and 3-38 characterize groundwater quality conditions within the Subbasin. Table 3-2 provides an additional quantitative summary for groundwater quality within the Subbasin. Water quality concentrations are compared to the state drinking water standards called maximum contaminant levels (MCLs). Not all constituents (e.g., TDS and chloride) have a primary MCL and are compared to the secondary MCLs (SMCLs) that address aesthetics such as taste and odor.
Localized historical exceedances of drinking water standards have been documented in the Subbasin for various constituents including exceedances of primary MCLs for nitrate and arsenic in areas of the Subbasin. Nitrate as nitrogen concentrations exceeding the MCL of 10 mg/L occur regionally in and around the cities Dixon and Vacaville, and also in the vicinity of Davis and Fairfield, which are not within the Solano Subbasin (Figure 3-13). More localized high nitrate areas also exist around several regulated facilities (i.e., contamination sites) within the Subbasin. Nitrate is a groundwater contaminant that is commonly associated with agricultural fertilizer application or leaching from septic and wastewater systems. Arsenic exceedances of the MCL of 10 μg/L are most common in the more southern parts of the Subbasin. The high arsenic levels in wells in the Subbasin are believed to be a result of naturally occurring arsenic in the local geologic materials. TDS concentrations in parts of the Subbasin are above the recommended secondary drinking water MCL of 500 mg/L with most areas having TDS concentrations below the upper secondary MCL of 750 mg/L. Elevated TDS concentrations are most commonly associated with groundwater occurring and moving within marine geologic deposits. Chromium-6 concentrations are elevated in some areas in the northern Subbasin, although most concentrations are below the MCL for total chromium of 50 μg/L. No applicable MCL specific to chromium-6 is in effect. The elevated chromium-6 levels are believed to be primarily from natural rock sources and geochemical processes. Boron levels in the Subbasin typically range from 0.35 to 0.7 mg/L throughout the Subbasin with some more local areas of higher concentrations greater than 0.7 mg/L in the southern Subbasin. No drinking water MCL exists for boron, although some crop and vegetation types are sensitive to elevated boron levels. Various local historical or ongoing contamination sites exist across the Subbasin, although no large-scale contamination plumes are known. Appendix 3-A Figures 3- 38 and 3-39 from the HCM TM show open and closed groundwater contamination sites within the Subbasin. A well permit consultation area has been delineated by the Regional Water Quality Control Board (RWQCB) in the vicinity of the City of Dixon where historical nitrate contamination of groundwater exists.
&&&
2.4.4. Migration of Contaminated Groundwater
The GeoTracker website identified approximately 260 potential groundwater contamination sites in the Solano Subbasin, including 34 sites associated with former military operations. Nearly 70% of all sites were designated as leaking underground tank cleanups and the remainders as cleanup or another  program. Over 80% of all sites were classified as closed or eligible for closure (3), including 4 sites with land use restrictions. Nearly 50 sites remain open with the status of inactive (4), assessment (27), remediation (13), or verification monitoring (5).
The Dixon Business Park is an open remediation site within the City 1089 of Dixon due to nitrate  contamination of groundwater from a former stockyard meat processing facility with unlined wastewater disposal ponds. The Regional Water Board has established a consultation zone (Figure 2-18)  for the site that extends into the unincorporated region 1,000 feet eastward (Brusca Associates, 2020). The Consultation Zone requires Solano County and Regional Water Board coordination and input from the Regional Water Board during the permitting process on the location and design of any new potable  water supply wells proposed within the zone. If the City of Dixon wants to construct a new well within the Consultation Zone, additional review and approval will be required from the Regional Water Board and Solano County. In the future, the Consultation Zone may be removed if monitoring data show that  the nitrate levels are no longer a risk. There are a few other sites in the Solano Subbasin where groundwater use is restricted due to residual contamination. These sites have conditional use restrictions or recorded covenants (see EnviroStor for site with land use restrictions).

3.5 General Groundwater Chemistry
The general groundwater chemistry in the upper 500 feet of the Subbasin was characterized by Thomasson et al. (1960) as magnesium bicarbonate to calcium magnesium bicarbonate. Sodium comprises between 10 and 40 percent of total cations and bicarbonate makes up between 70 and 95 percent of total anions (Thomason et al., 1960).
3.5.1 Total Dissolved Solids Concentrations
Maximum historical observed TDS concentrations for wells in and around the Subbasin are shown on Figure 3-21 and historical monitoring is summarized in Table 3-3. TDS values throughout the Subbasin range from less than 250 mg/L to greater than 1,000 mg/L. As illustrated in Figure 3-21, TDS
concentrations tend to be lower in the more northern parts of the Subbasin with an increasing number of wells with higher TDS concentrations occurring in more southern parts of the Subbasin. Most of the wells in the Subbasin have a maximum historical TDS concentration that is below the upper MCL of 1,000 mg/L and many of the wells in the northern and central Subbasin have TDS concentrations below the recommended MCL of 500 mg/L. Localized areas of higher TDS concentrations in groundwater correspond to environmental monitoring wells at regulated sites with potential for point source impacts to TDS concentrations. These regulated facilities within the Subbasin include a waste and organics disposal site (located along Highway 113 in the center of the subbasin), a concentrated animal feeding operation (CAFO) (located along Highway 12 north of Montezuma Hills), and a drilling mud waste  disposal facility (located along Highway 113 north of Montezuma Hills). Patterns in the historical values of specific conductance in the Subbasin are shown on Figure 3-22, which largely mimic the patterns exhibited for TDS.
3.5.2 Chloride Concentrations
Historical chloride concentrations in groundwater in the Subbasin are relatively low in most areas as displayed on Figure 3-23. The summary of monitoring for chloride in the Subbasin is presented in Table 3-4. Chloride concentrations in the northern Subbasin are typically less than 50 mg/L with nearly all well  results suggesting concentrations below 100 mg/L. An area of relatively higher chloride concentrations is evident in the central and western part of the Subbasin, likely related to the geologic materials of marine origin that occur at shallower depths or at the surface along and to the west 1499 of the Subbasin in this area. Except for a few notable regulated facility sites mentioned above where high TDS concentrations also exist, chloride concentrations within the Subbasin are generally below the secondary MCL of 250 mg/L. The generally low chloride concentrations across the Subbasin suggest little historical influence from any higher chloride concentrations that may have periodically occurred in the surface waterways of the Delta. Although elevated salinity and chloride concentrations have been observed in the Delta surface water during periods of major drought when freshwater outflows in the Delta were very low, no evidence of chronic intrusion of higher salinity surface water into the groundwater is apparent.
3.5.3 Nitrate Concentrations
Maximum historical nitrate concentrations are shown on Figure 3-24 and historical monitoring is summarized in Table 3-5. Nitrate concentrations in groundwater vary throughout the Subbasin from very low values below 2.5 mg/L (as N) to values exceeding the primary MCL of 10 mg/L. Nitrate
concentrations in the southern Subbasin tend to be relatively low (below 5 mg/L), although localized elevated nitrate concentrations above the MCL do exist in this part of the Subbasin. These localized areas of high nitrate concentrations include several regulated sites with historical point-source nitrate contamination. In the more northern parts of the Subbasin, higher nitrate concentrations exist along Interstate 80 between and around the Cities of Dixon and Davis. This area of elevated nitrate levels includes a number of wells exhibiting nitrate concentrations above the MCL. Somewhat limited data on nitrate concentrations are available to the north and northwest of Interstate 80, although most of the available results suggest relatively lower nitrate concentrations in this part of the Subbasin.
 3.5.4 Arsenic Concentrations
Maximum historical arsenic concentrations are shown on Figure 3-25 and historical monitoring is summarized in Table 3-6. Because of the natural hydrogeologic conditions, some notable areas of historically high arsenic concentrations in groundwater exist in parts of the Subbasin. Elevated arsenic concentrations are apparent in the more southern parts of the Subbasin where the occurrences of historical maximum arsenic concentrations above the MCL of 10 μg/L are more common. Although some local areas of elevated arsenic concentrations exist in the more northern parts of the Subbasin, the arsenic levels in groundwater in the northern Subbasin are commonly less than 5 μg/L with some localized areas or depth horizons of the aquifer system exhibiting higher concentrations, most notably in and around parts of Vacaville and Dixon. A groundwater quality study conducted by the USGS as part of the GAMA for the Southern Sacramento Valley, including the Solano Subbasin, found arsenic concentrations above the MCL in eight percent of wells sampled (Bennett et al., 2011). These higher concentrations are believed to be from natural sources and tended to occur near major river channels and in the Delta where naturally low dissolved oxygen concentrations in groundwater produce reducing geochemical conditions that increase the solubility of arsenic (Bennett et al., 2011).
3.5.5 Hexavalent Chromium 1535 (Chromium-6) Concentrations
Maximum historical concentrations of chromium-6 observed in sampled wells are shown on Figure 3-26 and historical monitoring is summarized in Table 3-7. Considerably less sampling data are available for chromium-6 than for some of the more common analytes of concern like TDS, nitrate, and chloride. Historical chromium-6 concentrations in the southern Subbasin are below 10 μg/L with the exception of one localized area around a regulated waste disposal site where concentrations are above 10 μg/L, including some results above the total chromium MCL of 50 μg/L. A greater number of wells with chromium-6 sample results above 10 μg/L exist farther north in the Subbasin, with the highest concentrations occurring in the north and eastern part of the Subbasin around Dixon and south of Davis. In the general vicinity of Davis along the Subbasin boundary, a number of historical results exceeding 50 μg/L are evident on Figure 3-26. As noted above, chromium occurs naturally in groundwater throughout California, including in the Subbasin, and can especially be associated with serpentinite rocks and other geologic formations containing chromium (SWRCB, 2017b). Serpentinite can be found in metamorphic and igneous rocks common in the Coast Ranges throughout northern California (Kruckeberg, 1985). The USGS recently found that elevated hexavalent chromium concentrations in groundwater contained in alluvial aquifers along the westside of the Central Valley are generally attributable to increased chromium availability in the source rock for those aquifers (Izbicki et al., 2015). Chromium can also occur in groundwater as result of localized contamination from industrial processes; however, chromium linked industrial processes are not associated with any regulated soil and groundwater remediation sites (i.e., GeoTracker sites) in the Subbasin, including in the vicinity of municipal production wells where chromium-6 concentrations have been detected at elevated levels as a result of natural geochemical processes that can enhance the occurrence of dissolved chromium-6, as observed by Chung et al. (2001) and Oze et al. (2007). Hexavalent chromium has been documented at varying depths within the groundwater system including in the Basal Tehama zone, likely as a result of the geochemical conditions.
3.5.6 Boron Concentrations
Maximum historical boron concentrations are shown on Figure 3-27 and suggest that boron levels in groundwater are below the Notification Level for drinking water of 1.0 mg/L throughout most of the Subbasin, with some areas of elevated levels. The summary of monitoring for boron in the Subbasin is presented in Table 3-8. Boron concentrations in the northwestern part of the Subbasin tend to be the lowest with generally increasing concentrations to the south and east. In the northwestern Subbasin in the vicinity of and to the north of Vacaville, the maximum historical boron concentrations are less than 0.3 mg/L with somewhat higher concentrations to the east, although most of the historical data in this area still suggest boron concentrations below 0.7 mg/L with some wells exhibiting higher concentrations. In the south, particular in the areas around Rio Vista, north and south of Rio Vista, and near Walnut Grove, more elevated boron concentrations exist, including many wells with concentrations above 0.7 mg/L and also a number of wells with concentrations over the drinking water Notification Level of 1.0 mg/L. Boron occurs in seawater and can be found in evaporite deposits and other sedimentary deposits, including in estuarine or marine sediments deposited in a saline environment. The elevated boron concentrations in the southern Subbasin are likely related to this type of depositional environment.
3.5.7 Other Groundwater Quality Constituents
Maps of a variety of other groundwater quality constituents are presented in Appendix G. Many of these maps highlight distinct areas of local groundwater contamination that should be considered when evaluating potential groundwater quality impacts from implementation of projects and management actions to achieve sustainability. Wells with detections and exceedances for a variety of constituents, including anthropogenic contaminants like pesticides, solvents, and petroleum-related chemicals, are displayed in maps in Appendix G. Most notably, maps of DBCP, EDB, 1,2,3-TCP, naphthalene, and BTEX concentrations all indicate areas or locations with wells exceeding the respective drinking water MCLs. Additional contaminants such as aldicarb sulfone, atrazine, diazinon, simazine, and perchlorate have also been detected in areas of the Subbasin, although at concentrations below drinking water MCLs. Naturally occurring constituents such as uranium and manganese are also elevated in some wells with high uranium concentrations more apparent in the northern and central parts of the Subbasin and high manganese concentrations more common in the southeastern parts of the Subbasin. Uranium levels are generally below the MCL, except for one exceedance in the Delta area, whereas a greater number of manganese exceedances exist in the Subbasin. Appendix H summarizes analytical results from historical water quality sampling conducted as part of monitoring of groundwater quality by zone in the Subbasin.

Identifying and characterizing the nature of interactions between groundwater and surface water in the Subbasin and managing the Subbasin groundwater resources in a manner that avoids significant and unreasonable adverse impacts to surface water from depletions caused by groundwater pumping is a requirement of all GSPs. Surface water located in areas where groundwater is very shallow have the potential to be directly connected to the groundwater system with potential for regional groundwater pumping to deplete surface water. To evaluate potential for interconnection between groundwater and surface water, the depth to groundwater at multiple years between 2000 and 2018 were compared at locations along mapped surface water in the Subbasin. The minimum DTW over the historical years evaluated (2000 through 2018) were compared to surface water locations to identify potentially interconnected surface water and the results of the evaluation are presented in Figure 3-17. Areas where groundwater is typically found very close to the ground surface (at less than ten feet below ground surface) suggests a likely direct connection between the groundwater and surface water, although characterizing the nature of the connection (i.e., gaining or losing) commonly requires greater level of local analysis. As the DTW under the surface water increases from 10 to 20 feet, the potential for direct interconnection decreases and the surface water is more likely to be a losing reach. At depths of greater than 20 feet, groundwater is more likely to be disconnected from surface water. Conditions in the Subbasin relating to the occurrence of interconnected surface water presented in Figure 3-17 with additional discussion of their identification and characteristics provided in (Appendix 3- B) prepared in support of the GSP development.

&& DTW data were calculated for spring of 2000, 2005, 2010, 2015, and 2018. As noted in the TM on the Subbasin HCM and groundwater conditions (LSCE, 2021), spring 2000 represents a relatively high groundwater level condition in the historical hydrologic record at the end of a 6-year high precipitation period, as illustrated by a plot of the cumulative departure from the mean precipitation (Figure 3-1). Spring 2000 groundwater levels represent conditions during an above normal year after a period of six years that had generally above average precipitation, including five water years (1995-1999) characterized as wet according to the Sacramento River water year index categorization by DWR. Other years evaluated included an above normal year in 2005 after a shorter period of approximately average local precipitation conditions, a below normal year in 2010 after a period a relatively dry local precipitation conditions, a critical year in 2015 after a sequence of several dry or critical years, and a more recent below normal year in 2018 after a very wet year in 2017. Figure 3-1 provides details on water year types and yearly precipitation for the Subbasin.

Unnamed: 9: Surface water located in areas where groundwater is very shallow have the potential to be directly connected to the groundwater system with potential for regional groundwater pumping to deplete surface water. To evaluate potential for interconnection between groundwater and surface water, the depth to groundwater at multiple years between 2000 and 2018 (as described in Section 3) were compared at locations along mapped surface water in the Subbasin. Historical depth to water maps for the Subbasin (see Figures 3-2a through 3-2e) show similar patterns across different years, with most of the central and Delta regions of the Subbasin having depths to water of less than 10 feet. Consistently the western Subbasin boundary north of Vacaville and Montezuma Hills have depths of water greater than 50 feet and are therefore not likely connected to groundwater. In the northern part of the Subbasin, DTW steadily increased over time and, in 2015, the deepest water levels were observed with many portions of the area north of Midway Road greater than 30 feet after several historically dry years. In 2018, DTW in the area experienced a slight rebound following a wet water year. The minimum DTW over the historical years evaluated (2000 through 2018) were compared to surface water locations to identify potentially interconnected surface water and the results of the evaluation are presented in Figure 4-2. Areas where groundwater is typically found very close to the ground surface (at less than ten feet below ground surface) suggests a likely direct connection between the groundwater and surface water, although characterizing the nature of the connection (i.e., gaining or losing) commonly requires greater level of local analysis. As the DTW under the surface water increases from 10 to 20 feet, the potential for direct interconnection decreases and the surface water is more likely to be a losing reach. At depths of greater than 20 feet, groundwater is more likely to be disconnected from surface water. Figure 4-2 shows the natural and artificial channels in the Subbasin with the most conservative estimates of potential for interconnectivity. Most of the natural channels in the Subbasin are located west of Interstate 505 and show that interconnectivity is unlikely between groundwater and surface water. East of Interstate 505, the streams are probably disconnected from groundwater with minimum depth to groundwater values ranging from 20 to 50 feet. In much of the southern part of the Subbasin, groundwater is shallow, and connectivity becomes likely, including many areas where historical gaining surface water conditions may exist. Many artificial channels in the Subbasin may exhibit interconnectedness with groundwater as they are commonly located in areas where DTW is less than 20 feet, and the majority are located in areas where depth to water is less than 10 feet.

Potential vegetative GDEs identified in the NCCAG dataset cover approximately three percent of the Solano Subbasin (Figure 5-2 and Table 5-1). In contrast, the Solano HCP identifies about 28% of the Subbasin as having natural communities or vegetation, although as noted above, the Solano HCP data are not representative of current areas with natural communities or vegetation. The majority of the potential GDEs are found in the Delta portion of the Subbasin where many natural and artificial waterways converge or terminate. According to the NCCAG, the most common vegetation species types are bulrush (California, Hardstem, Three-square), oak species (Coast Live, Valley), and willow (Arroyo, Goodding’s, Narrowleaf). Bulrush is a native perennial plant that is commonly found in wetland areas, the plant provides nesting areas for waterfowl and can help in soil stabilization due to its dense root structure (Tillley, 2012). The maximum rooting depth for Bulrush ranges from 1.97 to 2.13 feet (TNC, 2018). The Subbasin has two types of oak trees, the Coast Live Oak and the Valley Oak, which are located primarily on the western edge of the Subbasin to the north of Vacaville. The maximum rooting depth for oak trees ranges from 24 to 35 feet. The third most common vegetation is the willow tree, including the Arroyo, Goodding’s, and Narrowleaf Willows. The rooting depth for willows is approximately 7 feet. The remaining species represent a small percentage (less than 0.2%) of the total potential GDEs.

The process for identifying and mapping the locations of GDEs within the Solano Subbasin includes use of the Natural Communities Commonly Associated with Groundwater (NCCAG) Dataset as a starting point to identify potential GDEs within the Subbasin. Additional available ecosystem and habitat data from the County, including from the Solano Habitat Conservation Plan (HCP) (LSA, 2012), were reviewed and compared to the NCCAG data. Recent land use changes may have altered the locations of ecosystems and habitat in the Subbasin, and this was evaluated through comparison of recent land use data to the ecological datasets. The resulting locations of ecosystem communities and their characteristics, including rooting depths, were related to historical groundwater conditions to identify potential GDEs. Figure 3-18 identifies the likely GDEs within the Subbasin based on the intersection of mapped natural ecosystem community areas and the minimum depth to water threshold of 30 feet based on snapshots over the 2000-2018 period. Additional discussion of their identification and characteristics provided in (Appendix 3-B) prepared in support of the GSP development. Review of GDE status will be ongoing as part of GSP development and implementation, the identified likely GDEs will serve as the focus for consideration of groundwater sustainability in the GSP. Addition or removal of mapped GDEs may occur based on input from stakeholders and specialists.

&& The potential GDE communities and critical habitats were compared with 2016 land use mapping data (Figure 5-5) by Land IQ (2020) and depth to groundwater maps for 2000, 2005, 2010, 2015, and 2018 to identify likely locations of GDE communities, where groundwater is sufficiently shallow to serve as a source of water to ecosystem communities (Figures 3-2a though 3-2e). Historical land uses in the Subbasin have greatly modified the landscape and some areas where vegetation communities were mapped historically have now been converted to urban or agricultural areas. As shown on Figure 5-5, agricultural land uses cover substantial portions of the Subbasin and included actively cultivated agricultural land, idle agricultural land and pasture. Urban areas also include considerable acreage in the vicinity of Vacaville, Dixon, and Rio Vista. Based on consideration of the locations of potential GDEs as mapped in the NCCAG dataset and other datasets in relation to current land uses, areas that have been converted to urban and agricultural land uses were removed from consideration as potential GDEs. The identification of likely GDEs in the Subbasin was conducted by overlaying areas where depth to groundwater is less than 30 feet and likely to exceed the general vegetation maximum rooting depth for the ecosystem communities in the Subbasin. Based on this comparison, Figure 5-6 identifies the likelyGDEs within the Subbasin based on the intersection of mapped natural ecosystem community areas and the minimum depth to water threshold of 30 feet based on snapshots over the 2000-2018 period. Review of GDE status will be ongoing as part of GSP development and implementation, the identified likely GDEs will serve as the focus for consideration of groundwater sustainability in the GSP. Addition or removal of mapped GDEs may occur based on input from stakeholders and specialists.

Solano County Water Agency (SCWA) has a long-term master water supply contract with DWR for water supply from the State Water Project (SWP) that currently expires in 2035 but is renewable. SCWA is a North of Delta SWP Contractor and receives SWP water via the North Bay Aqueduct (NBA), which is owned and operated by DWR to deliver wholesale water supply for municipal and industrial uses from the Barker Slough Pumping Plant in the Delta to Napa and Solano Counties. SCWA’s contract with DWR includes a maximum allocation of 47,756 AFY, known as Table A Water. Supplemental SWP water, called Advanced Table A Water is available to SCWA under specific conditions. Additional supplemental water, Settlement Water (SW), is also available from year to year with some restrictions. For the purposes of the Solano Subbasin GSP, the SWP supply that is available is limited to Table A Water and North of Delta Allocation for Table A described as follows.

The Solano Subbasin is not located adjacent to the Pacific Ocean and that vast quantity of seawater. As such, seawater intrusion is not directly applicable to the Solano Subbasin. Nevertheless, the southeastern boundary of the Solano Subbasin is located in the northern portion of the Sacramento-San Joaquin River Delta and potential impacts could conceivably occur on limited occasion as a result of intrusion of higher-salinity surface water from San Pablo Bay.
&&
The Subbasin does not have coastline, but it is located within the Delta, where brackish surface water conditions exist as a result of tidal influences from the San Francisco Bay. The Solano Subbasin is in an area of the Delta characterized primarily as a tidal fresh zone with some smaller areas characterized as mixing zone (Appendix 3-B). As noted previously, groundwater use in the southern parts of the Solano Subbasin is very limited with most of the use consisting of domestic wells for private residences. Historical chloride concentrations in groundwater do not indicate any notable areas of likely historical intrusion of higher salinity water from Delta surface waterways. Chloride concentrations in groundwater in the Subbasin are generally less than 50 mg/L, which is well below the secondary MCL of 250 mg/L.

Unnamed: 9: The small magnitude of potential change in streamflow exhibited in the Solano Subbasin surface water features under the projected future conditions, including with climate change, suggests that it is unlikely that any beneficial users of surface water would be significantly and unreasonably adversely affected by groundwater management under any of the projected future conditions evaluated. Similarly, changes in subsurface flows under the projected scenarios are small and will not impede the ability of adjacent subbasins to achieve or maintain sustainability. Accordingly, for the purpose of the GSP, the sustainable yield is estimated to be 190,000 acre-feet per year, which is equal to the volume of groundwater extracted annually in the Subbasin under the projected model scenario with future land use and 2070 climate change conditions. This volume is approximately equal to the annual volume of deep percolation occurring within the Subbasin, which serves to replenish groundwater storage, in addition to other water budget inflows. Assuming potential uncertainty of 20 percent associated with the water budget estimates, an associated range of values for the estimated sustainable yield would be 150,000 to 230,000 acre-feet per year. Of the total sustainable yield estimated for the Subbasin, the sustainable yield within the Basal Tehama Zone is estimated to be about 8,300 acre-feet per year based on water budget results by primary aquifer (see Table 5-25). It is possible that the true sustainable yield for the Subbasin is higher as no model scenarios were developed to test the maximum possible volume of groundwater extraction. The sustainable yield estimate provided here is consistent with the sustainability goal for the Subbasin and will be reviewed as the Subbasin implements the GSP, including through periodic review and updates to the Solano IHM and water budget results, ongoing monitoring of Subbasin conditions as required by GSP Regulations, and filling of any data gaps identified in the GSP or during GSP implementation.

6.2.1.1. Criteria to Define Undesirable Results
The definition of undesirable results (URs) for chronic lowering of groundwater levels is excessive regional groundwater pumping that causes a significant and unreasonable decline in groundwater levels over an extended period of time that results in existing water supply wells (drinking water, industrial, and irrigation wells) not being viable for intended beneficial uses due to reduction in pumping capacity, or groundwater levels exhibit lowering that significantly affects other beneficial uses (e.g., GDEs). An undesirable result for chronic lowering of groundwater levels is lowering of groundwater levels across a large enough area and at a magnitude to result in significant and unreasonable adverse impacts to beneficial uses and users in the Subbasin. The GSAs determined that such an undesirable condition exists when 30 percent or more of representative monitoring site (RMS) wells exceed their respective groundwater level MT for two consecutive years, indicating a persistent lowered condition that could significantly and unreasonably adversely impact beneficial uses and users of groundwater in the Subbasin. The use of a two-year consecutive exceedance provides a good balance between providing tolerance for some short-term extreme groundwater demands while not allowing for long-term excessive pumping of groundwater. Use of a 30 percent criterion for RMS MT exceedances constituting an undesirable result provides an appropriate balance between recognizing the potential occurrence of localized conditions versus conditions representative of a more significant fraction of the Subbasin. The occurrence of any UR would be evaluated with respect to the causes for such conditions and the relationship to sustainable management activities occurring within the Subbasin.
&&&
6.2.1.3. Potential Effects of Undesirable Results
A potential adverse effect of the chronic lowering of groundwater levels includes impacts to domestic well owners caused by lowered groundwater levels that result in domestic wells being unable to produce sufficient water for drinking water purposes. Another potential adverse effect would be excessive lowering of shallow groundwater levels near GDEs that result in significant degradation of these ecosystem communities. These changes could impact groundwater users’ human right to water, local property values, quality of life, and ecosystem function in the Subbasin. Impacts would be concentrated in the shallower part of the groundwater system, primarily the widely used Alluvial Aquifer/Upper Tehama Zone. The Basal Tehama Zone, which is generally very deep in the Subbasin and is used to a limited degree by municipal wells, is not used as a source of supply to domestic well users or GDEs. The Basal Tehama is a highly confined aquifer and, therefore, can exhibit considerable variability in water levels in response to very limited change in storage. As a result, changes in groundwater levels are not indicative of substantial loss of groundwater storage. Groundwater level declines in the Basal Tehama would impact municipal groundwater supply reliability with lowered piezometric heads causing need for increased pumping lift and associated power demands resulting in increased costs for municipal water supply. Variability in piezometric head in the Basal Tehama is not likely to present a significant and
unreasonable adverse impact in the Subbasin as significant lowering of groundwater levels does not equate to significant loss in storage and the impacts from such an occurrence are limited to the increased cost of lift to municipal well operators who are the only beneficial user of the aquifer. The relationship between groundwater levels and changes in groundwater storage are discussed in Section 6.2.2.
&&&
6.2.4.2. Potential Causes of Undesirable Results
Overall, groundwater quality is satisfactory for the different beneficial uses present in the Subbasin. However, potential causes of degraded groundwater quality may include the following: • Changes in baseline water quality at any location which indicate new pathways or mechanisms of degradation of any freshwater source that adversely impacts existing beneficial uses and users. • Substantial and long-term changes to the location or rates of pumping, beyond the range of distribution and rate historically occurring in the Subbasin, could result in mobilization and migration of certain constituents including anthropogenic sources of contamination or natural constituents of concern, including connate groundwater. • Surface activities to enhance recharge (e.g., recharge basins) could cause localized groundwater mounding to alter flow directions and potential movement of water quality constituents towards wells in concentrations that exceed water quality standards. Recharge of water that would lead to exceeding the maximum contaminant level (MCL) or secondary MCL (SMCL) causing water quality degradation.
&&&
6.2.4.3. Potential Effects of Undesirable Results
Degradation of water quality in the Subbasin could impact the use of groundwater for domestic, municipal, and agricultural purposes. The constituents of concern and the associated MCLs for drinking water (e.g., domestic, municipal) are generally protective of environmental uses of groundwater in the Subbasin. Although some localized groundwater quality contamination exists along with some elevated concentrations of natural constituents, historically there have been no significant limitations on the suitability of groundwater quality for use as supply for any groundwater beneficial uses in the Subbasin. For localized areas historically impacted by degraded groundwater quality from anthropogenic activities (e.g., regulated facility contamination sites), the GSAs expect to coordinate with the agencies and programs with regulatory jurisdiction over such issues to ensure groundwater management does not exacerbate degraded groundwater quality conditions and support mitigation and remediation efforts overseen by regulatory programs. The potential effects of URs for degradation of water quality are: • Inability to ensure the human right to safe, clean, affordable and reliable water to the subbasin’s communities. • Reduced available water supply caused by degraded water quality conditions requiring users to replace wells or seek alternative sources of supply. • Economic hardships on domestic wells users requiring installation of water treatment or alternative sources. • Added costs to municipalities to install treatment systems or seek alternative sources. • Adverse effects to groundwater dependent ecosystems due to changes in quality. • Adverse effects on property values for landowners that rely on groundwater for domestic and agricultural supply.
&&&&

6.2.1.1. Criteria to Define Undesirable Results
The definition of undesirable results (URs) for chronic lowering of groundwater levels is excessive regional groundwater pumping that causes a significant and unreasonable decline in groundwater levels over an extended period of time that results in existing water supply wells (drinking water, industrial, and irrigation wells) not being viable for intended beneficial uses due to reduction in pumping capacity, or groundwater levels exhibit lowering that significantly affects other beneficial uses (e.g., GDEs). An undesirable result for chronic lowering of groundwater levels is lowering of groundwater levels across a large enough area and at a magnitude to result in significant and unreasonable adverse impacts to beneficial uses and users in the Subbasin. The GSAs determined that such an undesirable condition exists when 30 percent or more of representative monitoring site (RMS) wells exceed their respective groundwater level MT for two consecutive years, indicating a persistent lowered condition that could significantly and unreasonably adversely impact beneficial uses and users of groundwater in the Subbasin. The use of a two-year consecutive exceedance provides a good balance between providing tolerance for some short-term extreme groundwater demands while not allowing for long-term excessive pumping of groundwater. Use of a 30 percent criterion for RMS MT exceedances constituting an undesirable result provides an appropriate balance between recognizing the potential occurrence of localized conditions versus conditions representative of a more significant fraction of the Subbasin. The occurrence of any UR would be evaluated with respect to the causes for such conditions and the relationship to sustainable management activities occurring within the Subbasin.
&&&
6.2.1.3. Potential Effects of Undesirable Results
A potential adverse effect of the chronic lowering of groundwater levels includes impacts to domestic well owners caused by lowered groundwater levels that result in domestic wells being unable to produce sufficient water for drinking water purposes. Another potential adverse effect would be excessive lowering of shallow groundwater levels near GDEs that result in significant degradation of these ecosystem communities. These changes could impact groundwater users’ human right to water, local property values, quality of life, and ecosystem function in the Subbasin. Impacts would be concentrated in the shallower part of the groundwater system, primarily the widely used Alluvial Aquifer/Upper Tehama Zone. The Basal Tehama Zone, which is generally very deep in the Subbasin and is used to a limited degree by municipal wells, is not used as a source of supply to domestic well users or GDEs. The Basal Tehama is a highly confined aquifer and, therefore, can exhibit considerable variability in water levels in response to very limited change in storage. As a result, changes in groundwater levels are not indicative of substantial loss of groundwater storage. Groundwater level declines in the Basal Tehama would impact municipal groundwater supply reliability with lowered piezometric heads causing need for increased pumping lift and associated power demands resulting in increased costs for municipal water supply. Variability in piezometric head in the Basal Tehama is not likely to present a significant and unreasonable adverse impact in the Subbasin as significant lowering of groundwater levels does not equate to significant loss in storage and the impacts from such an occurrence are limited to the increased cost of lift to municipal well operators who are the only beneficial user of the aquifer. The relationship between groundwater levels and changes in groundwater storage are discussed in Section 6.2.2.
&&&
6.2.4.2. Potential Causes of Undesirable Results
Overall, groundwater quality is satisfactory for the different beneficial uses present in the Subbasin. However, potential causes of degraded groundwater quality may include the following: • Changes in baseline water quality at any location which indicate new pathways or mechanisms of degradation of any freshwater source that adversely impacts existing beneficial uses and users. • Substantial and long-term changes to the location or rates of pumping, beyond the range of distribution and rate historically occurring in the Subbasin, could result in mobilization and migration of certain constituents including anthropogenic sources of contamination or natural constituents of concern, including connate groundwater. • Surface activities to enhance recharge (e.g., recharge basins) could cause localized groundwater mounding to alter flow directions and potential movement of water quality constituents towards wells in concentrations that exceed water quality standards. Recharge of water that would lead to exceeding the maximum contaminant level (MCL) or secondary MCL (SMCL) causing water quality degradation.
&&&
6.2.4.3. Potential Effects of Undesirable Results
Degradation of water quality in the Subbasin could impact the use of groundwater for domestic, municipal, and agricultural purposes. The constituents of concern and the associated MCLs for drinking water (e.g., domestic, municipal) are generally protective of environmental uses of groundwater in the Subbasin. Although some localized groundwater quality contamination exists along with some elevated concentrations of natural constituents, historically there have been no significant limitations on the suitability of groundwater quality for use as supply for any groundwater beneficial uses in the Subbasin. For localized areas historically impacted by degraded groundwater quality from anthropogenic activities (e.g., regulated facility contamination sites), the GSAs expect to coordinate with the agencies and programs with regulatory jurisdiction over such issues to ensure groundwater management does not exacerbate degraded groundwater quality conditions and support mitigation and remediation efforts overseen by regulatory programs. The potential effects of URs for degradation of water quality are: • Inability to ensure the human right to safe, clean, affordable and reliable water to the subbasin’s communities. • Reduced available water supply caused by degraded water quality conditions requiring users to replace wells or seek alternative sources of supply. • Economic hardships on domestic wells users requiring installation of water treatment or alternative sources. • Added costs to municipalities to install treatment systems or seek alternative sources. • Adverse effects to groundwater dependent ecosystems due to changes in quality. • Adverse effects on property values for landowners that rely on groundwater for domestic and agricultural supply.
&&&&
6.2.6.1. Criteria to Define Undesirable Results
The definition of URs for depletion of interconnected surface water is excessive regional groundwater pumping that results in reductions in flow or stage of major surface water features that are hydrologically connected to groundwater in the Subbasin, and which cause significant and unreasonable impacts to beneficial uses and users of surface water (e.g., surface water rights holders, environmental users/GDEs). SGMA requires each GSP to consider the consequences of URs even if they have not occurred historically or are not projected to occur in the future. Locally-defined significant and unreasonable conditions were based on discussion with GSA staff and technical representatives, input received from interested stakeholders and the public through community meetings and other input  mechanisms, and through individual stakeholder input to various GSA representatives. The SMC metric for URs are the streamflows along Putah Creek and groundwater levels as a proxy elsewhere. The Subbasin has defined URs for interconnected surface water as a condition when either the flow requirements and other conditions stipulated in the Putah Creek Accord are not met or maintained or 30 percent of RMS wells associated with monitoring of interconnected surface water experience water levels below the MT for two consecutive years as a result of excessive pumping occurring within the Subbasin. Use of the Putah Creek Accord and 30 percent criterion for RMS MT exceedances constituting an undesirable result provides an appropriate balance between recognizing  the potential occurrence of localized conditions versus conditions representative of a more significant fraction of the Subbasin. The occurrence of any UR would be evaluated with respect to the causes for such conditions and the relationship to sustainable management activities occurring within the Subbasin.
&&&
6.2.6.2. Potential Causes of Undesirable Results
Potential causes of depletion of interconnected surface water include the following: • New large-scale pumping or concentrated pumping from densely located shallow wells. • New localized pumping in locations where surface water is connected to the Alluvial Aquifer and Upper Tehama Zone.  • Interception or reduction of groundwater discharge to surface water.
&&&
6.2.6.3. Potential Effects of Undesirable Results
Depletions of interconnected surface water could result in: • Reduction in streamflows that negatively impact environmental users, including groundwater  dependent ecosystems. • Reduced flows within rivers and streams that adversely impact 688 diversions for agricultural or urban users. • Increased costs to mitigate impacts.

The MTs for chronic lowering of groundwater levels are based on a selection of RMS wells from existing and planned near-term future monitoring wells located throughout the Subbasin and screened in the two primary aquifers: the Alluvial Aquifer and Upper Tehama Zone, and the Basal Tehama. In the Alluvial Aquifer and Upper Tehama Zone, the setting of MTs for groundwater levels first considered the minimum static groundwater elevation over the historical base period (water year 1991 to prior to January 2015), when a sufficiently long data record exists and where this level is anticipated to provide sufficient operational flexibility in the Subbasin without causing adverse impacts to beneficial users. In the northwestern area, particular consideration was given to the depth of nearby domestic wells when evaluating appropriate MTs in this area. Consideration was also given to GDEs at RMS wells representative of conditions near GDEs. Where the historical low groundwater level over the base period does not provide sufficient operational flexibility to accommodate drought conditions and intermittent periods of higher pumping, MTs were lowered through consideration of longer-term historical conditions (prior to and after the base period) together with levels needed to avoid significant and unreasonable adverse impacts on beneficial users in the vicinity of each RMS. For wells constructed in the highly confined Basal Tehama Zone, groundwater levels are reflective of potentiometric head which produce higher magnitudes of fluctuations in response to small changes in storage. In these wells, a similar review of historical groundwater level data was conducted and MTs were generally set at a level 50 feet below the recent five-year average (prior to January 2015) groundwater level to allow sufficient operational flexibility for beneficial users of groundwater from the Basal Tehama Zone, which are entirely municipal suppliers. The top of the Basal Tehama Zone generally occurs at depths of 1,000 feet or more across most of the Subbasin (where present) and declines in the potentiometric head of 50 feet correspond with a change in storage of about 500 acre-feet and will not significantly or unreasonably impact beneficial users of the aquifer. The selected RMS wells for the groundwater level sustainability indicator are listed in Table 6-3 and shown on Figures 6-1a and 6-1b. Locations of supplemental water level monitoring conducted within the Subbasin on a regular basis are also displayed on Figures 6-1a and 6-1b. Water level monitoring at supplemental sites will continue in the Subbasin to inform regular evaluation and reporting on groundwater conditions. Historical groundwater level hydrographs for RMS wells, including their respective MTs, are provided in Appendix 6A.
&&&
6.3.1.1. Information and Criteria Relied Upon to Establish MTs
The methodology to develop MTs for chronic decline of groundwater levels was based on discussion with GSA staff and technical representatives, input received from interested stakeholders and the public through community meetings and other public input mechanisms, through individual stakeholder input to various GSA representatives, and review of SGMA GSP regulations and DWR BMPs (DWR, 2016). The DWR Well Completion Report (WCR) database was reviewed to evaluate the locations and construction details of various types of wells. There were several steps involved with identifying RMS wells and determining groundwater level MTs as follows: 1) Evaluate the DWR WCR database for the location and construction details of wells historically or currently monitored in the Subbasin (as described above).
2) Review available existing and likely future wells with regard to several variables/criteria (e.g., GSA ownership and/or access to well, known well construction details, preference for wells with multiple years of historical water level data, availability of recent water level data, good spatial distribution) and select appropriate RMS locations. 3) Historical groundwater elevations from supplemental monitoring wells in the Subbasin. 4) Depths and locations of existing nearby water supply wells. 5) Proximity and relationship to environmental beneficial users. 6) Current and historical groundwater elevation contour maps. 7) Simulated water levels for the Subbasin, including historical simulations and projected simulations with climate change. 8) Other information from GSAs and interested parties regarding significant and unreasonable Conditions. Some of the key characteristics considered during development of the MTs at each RMS are highlighted in Table 6-3, including proximity to GDEs and depth and density of nearby domestic wells. Example hydrographs showing MTs are provided in Figures 6-2 through 6-4. The hydrograph for RMS 06N01E12M001M (Figure 6-2) 07N01E29P001M (Figure 6-3) and MW-15-1815ft (Figure 6-4) demonstrate the MTs for Alluvial Aquifer, Upper Tehama and Basal Tehama, respectively.
&&&
6.3.1.4. How the MT may Affect the Interests of Beneficial Uses and Users of
Groundwater RMS wells were selected to provide a basis for evaluating changes and impacts to the different uses and users of water wells throughout the Subbasin.
• Rural residential land uses and users. The chronic lowering of groundwater level MTs protects most domestic users of groundwater by considering the depths to which wells are completed and protection of reasonable operating margins for available pumping drawdown. Water level MTs were set with specific consideration of domestic well depths in the vicinity of RMS wells. A comparison of a hypothetical MT water surface developed by interpolating MT values between RMS wells to potential domestic well locations based on DWR WCR data (wells constructed with known construction). The locations and construction of wells that are currently active in the Subbasin is not knowns and some older WCRs may be associated with wells that are no longer active wells. The analysis relating to the interpolated MT surface was constrained to the area with RMS wells, which was developed with consideration of monitoring locations interpreted to be representative of regional conditions versus localized conditions. Figure 6-5 illustrates how if this hypothetical condition occurred if the future, review of domestic wells-constructed since 1970 suggests that two (or 0.17%) of the domestic wells in the Subbasin have the potential to go dry i.e., the well had less than 10 feet of saturated screen. For wells constructed since 1980, the review suggests that one well (or 0.13%) has the potential to go dry. In reality, a UR occurs when 30 percent of RMS wells exceed the water level MT, whereas this hypothetical surface represents a condition when MTs are reached at 100 percent of RMS. Although this comparison is highly conservative in evaluating potential impacts to domestic well users, it shows how the MTs are set to avoid significant and unreasonable impacts on these beneficial users.
Urban land uses and users. The chronic lowering of groundwater level MTs are set so that  municipal groundwater pumpers can meet existing and projected water demands by protecting their ability to meet existing and projected demands through typical well and pumping configurations (e.g., depths, perforation intervals, pumping lifts).

6.3.3.1. Information and Criteria Relied Upon to Establish MTs
The methodology to develop MTs for groundwater quality is based on the objective of protecting all designated beneficial uses from significant and unreasonable adverse impacts from implementation of GSP projects and management actions. In accordance with the Basin Plan (RWQCB, 2018), groundwater in the Subbasin is considered suitable or potentially suitable for municipal and domestic water supply, agricultural supply, industrial service supply, and industrial process supply beneficial uses. From a groundwater quality standpoint, the municipal and domestic supply beneficial uses are the most restrictive with Basin Plan water quality objectives linked to drinking water MCLs. As a result, the MTs for groundwater quality set for each of the five identified key water quality constituents (nitrate, arsenic, TDS, chloride, Cr6) are the respective MCL values, except for Cr6 where there is currently no MCL, the MT is set at current guidelines for 10 ug/L. When the baseline concentration exceeds the MCL or guidelines the MT is determined to be the baseline concentration plus 20 percent. When current or historical water quality for the key constituents has not been measured, the MT will be set as the MCL and will be adjusted if needed after water quality monitoring commences. The applicable MTs for groundwater quality in the GSP apply to degraded groundwater quality as a direct result of impacts from projects or management actions under the GSP that cause an exceedance to occur. Future exceedances of the MT may occur due to activities or conditions unrelated to implementation of the GSP, in which case they would not constitute an MT exceedance that contributes to an undesirable result. An example groundwater quality RMS wells time series for TDS is provided in Figure 6-7.
&&&
6.3.3.4. How the MT May Affect the Interests of Beneficial Uses and Users of Groundwater
Municipal and domestic supplies are the most restrictive beneficial uses for groundwater quality with water quality objectives equal to drinking water MCLs. Setting the groundwater quality MTs for key  constituent concentrations at respective drinking water MCLs, or within a tolerance for no more than a 20 percent increase above historical concentrations when existing or historical concentrations already exceed the MCL, is intended to limit degradation of groundwater quality caused by SGMA-related  groundwater management activities and GSP projects and management actions and protect municipal and domestic supply beneficial uses. Protection of municipal and domestic beneficial uses is also protective of other groundwater beneficial uses.
6.3.3.5. How the MT Relates to the Federal, State, or Local Standards
The Federal and State drinking water quality standards are represented through MCLs that are applicable to public drinking water supplies and provide reasonable guidance on water quality for safe drinking water in non-public supplies. As described above, the State of California drinking water MCLs for arsenic, nitrate, chloride, and TDS are being used to define MTs for groundwater quality degradation caused by GSP projects and management actions, except in cases where existing or historical groundwater quality conditions already exceed these levels.

Putah Creek is regulated to ensure that there is adequate water to serve the various GDEs found along the Creek. The Putah Creek Accord (Accord) summarizes the flows required in the Creek. The Accord will serve as MTs for Putah Creek, compliance monitoring points, and requirements are identified on Figure 6-10 and Table 6-6. As identified in the ISW GDE TM (GSP Section 3 Appendix 3-B), surface water features in the central to southeastern portions of the Subbasin are identified as connected likely connected; therefore, areas determined to be connected or likely connected, i.e., depth to water less than 20 feet, have MTs. Additionally, there are five shallow wells located along Putah Creek and in the Delta that are proposed to be included in the RMS network. The groundwater levels will serve as a proxy for the other smaller surface water features and the MT will be the minimum observed static groundwater elevation in the base period prior to 2015, RMS sites are identified on Figure 6-10 and summarized in Table 6-7.

6.3.5.1. Information and Criteria 1189 Relied Upon to Establish MTs
1190 Multiple flow and stream gage monitoring locations exist in the Subbasin and surrounding areas, and these have a long history of monitoring conditions. The stations are monitored by DWR, USGS, and SCWA. Many of the stations are located in the Delta area and a smaller amount are spread throughout the Subbasin to monitor Putah Creek, Alamo Creek, Sweeny Creek, Ulatis Creek, and the various water carrying canals. Historical data associated with these stations provide data to develop historical baselines that can be compared with future monitoring results. Smaller streams are connected to groundwater south of Highway 80. These streams, however, do not have flow meters and only relative stream stage heights. This indicate that the stage heights are relatively low and fairly constant with some stages capturing precipitation events and agricultural water supply. For all the interconnected surface waters, GDE distribution/viability and reduction in surface water availability were considered. The ISW GDE TM (GSP Section 3 Appendix 3-B) provides details on GDE distribution throughout the Subbasin, and Figure 6-10 shows the likely GDEs in relation to the RMS locations. Putah Creek has long-standing historical requirements for flows, and it was determined the current regulations would be sufficient for MTs to allow for continual beneficial use and users of groundwater. It was determined that flows in the Delta are so large, and groundwater in that area is so shallow, that depletions are insignificant and no MT is needed at this time.

The selected MTs for surface water depletion will help protect beneficial uses/users that are dependent on streamflow, which may include potential GDEs along particular reaches of some creeks. However, beneficial users (i.e., well owners) pumping shallow groundwater near creeks may be impacted by having their pumping restricted due to the minimum thresholds for surface water depletion established under this GSP.

For constituents with a primary MCL MT, a Trigger Level is set at 75 percent of the MCL. The Trigger initiates evaluation of factors related to increasing constituent concentrations. MOs for the groundwater quality sustainability indicator are intended to ensure that GSP projects and  management actions do not cause groundwater quality conditions to become unsuitable for any beneficial use, especially municipal and domestic supply uses since these are the most restrictive from a water quality standpoint. Additionally, the MOs are intended to address additional potential  groundwater quality impacts associated with GSP projects and management actions that may result from lowered groundwater levels in some areas or altered groundwater flow dynamics. MO concentrations for groundwater quality for each sustainability indicator well are summarized in Table 6-9, and locations of groundwater quality sustainability indicator RMS wells are shown in Figure 6- 6. Tables and graphs of historical results for key water quality constituents in the groundwater quality indicator RMS wells are presented in Appendix 6B and an example is provided in Figure 6-7.

8.2.1. Multi-Benefit Recharge
Multi-benefit recharge projects are promising tools to maximize the benefits of recharge for numerous beneficial uses and users of groundwater. For the Solano Subbasin, these projects are focused on capturing and utilizing rainfall runoff on agricultural fields for managed aquifer recharge (MAR). In this GSP, multi-benefit recharge projects that strategically inundate fields with winter stormflows are referred to as “Flood-MAR” projects, whereas recharge projects that strategically capture and recharge rainfall on fields are referred to as “Rain-MAR” projects. The main goals of these multi-benefit recharge projects 190 are to simultaneously:  Recharge groundwater supplies using available surface water supplies and runoff, and Create temporary habitat for environmental water users, such as migratory shorebirds along the Pacific Flyway. These multi-benefit recharge projects would be implemented through participating growers that voluntarily flood their fields with available stormflows (Flood-MAR) or construct berms or sumps to capture and recharge rainfall runoff (Rain-MAR). These projects would contribute recharge to the aquifer system and also provide benefits to flood risk reduction, water quality improvement, climate  change adaptation, and ecosystem enhancement for wildlife, especially shorebirds during peak migratory periods.
Determination and consideration of community challenges and interests were led primarily by the Dixonmand Solano Resource Conservation Districts (RCDs) and Local Government Commission (LGC) through previous NRCS Conservation Innovation Grant (CIG) funding and Proposition 1 Severely Disadvantaged Communities (SDAC) engagement projects managed by The Freshwater Trust (TFT). The Dixon RCD also  serves as the drainage district and is able to provide important insights into the flooding challenges experienced in northern Solano County. Multiple grower interviews were conducted in 2019 and 2020 by TFT and others to determine community interest, need, and willingness to adopt specific farm management practices. The GSA Collaborative ultimately selected two multi-benefit recharge PMAs: the first is the "Westside Streams Stormwater Capture Project" and the second is the “Rainfall Managed Aquifer Recharge (Rain- MAR) project,” both of which are described in detail in Section 8.4.
&&&
8.5.1.2. The Nature Conservancy Multi-Benefit Groundwater Recharge Program
The Nature Conservancy (TNC) has provided GSAs with guidelines and support to implement on-farm, multi-benefit groundwater recharge programs. The program would build on the successful TNC BirdReturns program by strategically flooding agricultural fields with the dual goals of: (1) recharging groundwater supplies, while (2) simultaneously creating critical winter habitat for shorebirds migrating along the Pacific Flyway. GSAs could consider offering financial incentives to growers to compensate them for recharging groundwater through field flooding in the course of normal farming operations, with multiple potential benefits to the underlying aquifer system, waterbirds migrating along the Pacific Flyway, and other beneficial users of groundwater in the Subbasin. The program could be structured to pay for field preparation, irrigation, and water costs to encourage grower participation. Benefits to small  farmers could also be prioritized.  If implemented, the multi-benefit groundwater recharge program would occur in multiple phases in the  Solano Subbasin, with expansion of the program over time as voluntary grower participation increases. Multi-benefit recharge would be implemented at selected sites, with multi-benefits to groundwater recharge and temporary wetland habitat formation. Recharge and wetland habitat benefits in the early phases of the project would be analyzed, reported, and used to inform development and later implementation of the program.
Implementation of this project could commence with selection of sites suitable for multi-benefit recharge, and initiation of any necessary permitting and environmental documentation. GSAs could use resources in the GSP and also provided by TNC to identify fields with soil and cropping conditions  conducive to groundwater recharge and temporary wetland habitat formation. In later phases of project implementation, other suitable fields could continue to be identified following similar criteria, with refinement according to lessons learned from early project implementation.
&&&
